worth_it_explanation: ClearLine is the path that carries a publisher's direct buyer relationships and curated packages straight to demand, so its setup decides whether those deals reach the inventory they were built for and clear at the price the publisher intended. Curated packages that point at the wrong inventory, or that drift out of date as the site changes, quietly underdeliver while still looking active in the console. The check is a review of packages and mappings a publisher already has in Magnite, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal or package the publisher can correct.
intro: ClearLine is Magnite's tool for connecting a publisher's direct buyer demand and curated inventory packages to programmatic spend without routing through a separate demand-side platform. A curated package bundles inventory, audience signals, and content context into a deal a buyer can activate, so the value of the package depends on the bundle pointing at the right inventory and staying current. This subcategory is about the package and deal setup itself, what each package targets and whether it still matches the site, rather than the broader header-bidding or floor mechanics that carry the bids. These checks confirm that ClearLine direct demand and curated packages are mapped to the inventory they were meant for and that the packages are maintained rather than left to go stale.

audit_checks.title: ClearLine deals and curated packages are not mapped to the right inventory
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package or direct deal that targets the wrong inventory either fails to deliver, leaving the buyer relationship and the revenue it carries unfulfilled, or it serves inventory the buyer did not agree to, which erodes trust in the deal. Either way the package does not do the job it was set up for, so spend the publisher expected to clear through ClearLine clears somewhere else or not at all.

educate.base:
ClearLine is the route Magnite gives a publisher to bring direct buyer demand and curated inventory packages to programmatic spend without handing the relationship to a separate demand-side platform. A curated package is a bundle. It joins a slice of inventory, an audience or content signal, and a price into a deal that a buyer can activate. The value of that bundle rests on one thing above the rest, which is that the inventory it points at is the inventory the buyer was promised.

The mapping can drift away from that promise in ordinary ways. A package built for a publisher's video inventory may still target an ad-unit grouping that was renamed or restructured, so it now reaches less of the video supply than intended, or reaches display units the buyer never agreed to. A geographic or audience targeting layer set up for one campaign may carry over to a package where it does not belong. A deal scoped to premium placements may, after an inventory reorganization, include filler units that pull the average delivery below the price the buyer expects. None of this surfaces on its own. The deal still shows as active in the ClearLine console while quietly delivering against the wrong supply.

The buyer relationship is the asset here, and a deal that misdelivers spends that asset down. A buyer who agreed to a curated package and then receives inventory that does not match it has a reason to question the next package, so a mapping error costs more than the impressions involved. Supply chain transparency standards exist to let buyers verify the seller and the path their spend travels, which raises the stakes on a package pointing where it claims to.

A few signs point to packages that are not mapped to the right inventory:
  • A curated package or deal targets an ad-unit grouping that has been renamed, merged, or restructured since the package was built
  • Delivery reporting shows a package serving inventory types, formats, or placements that do not match what the buyer agreed to
  • A premium-scoped deal includes filler or remnant inventory that an inventory reorganization swept in
  • Targeting layers from an unrelated campaign carry over to a package where they do not belong
You may want to confirm that each active ClearLine package and deal still targets the inventory it was built for. One approach is to open each package, compare its targeting against the inventory the buyer agreed to, and read delivery reporting to see what it is serving against in practice.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages are often built around editorial sections, a video player, or a sponsorship-adjacent placement, and those structures change as the site is reorganized. You may want to recheck that section-based or video packages still target the inventory they name after any restructuring, so a package sold as premium-section supply does not quietly start serving article-footer units.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a curated package may be built around product or category pages where the audience sits closer to a purchase and the inventory commands a higher price. You may want to confirm that the package still targets the commercial pages specifically rather than a broader grouping that an inventory change folded the editorial pages into, which would dilute what the buyer agreed to with editorial supply.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed setup documentation for ClearLine deals and curated packages is in the partner portal (login required), so the public links above cover the underlying deal and supply-chain standards rather than the Magnite console steps.

investigate.intro: Review each active ClearLine deal and curated package against the inventory it was built for, then read delivery reporting to see what it is serving against in practice.

magnite_console.label: Magnite ClearLine console
steps: Open the ClearLine section of the Magnite console and list the active deals and curated packages.
For each package, open it and record the inventory it targets, including the ad-unit groupings, formats, geographies, and any audience or content signals layered on.

Compare each package's targeting against the inventory the buyer agreed to, and note any package that points at a renamed, merged, or restructured grouping, or that carries a targeting layer from an unrelated campaign.
Flag any premium-scoped deal whose targeting now includes filler or remnant inventory swept in by an inventory change.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a delivery report broken out by deal or package, with the inventory, format, and placement it served against.
Compare what each package served against to the inventory it was scoped for, and note any package delivering against inventory types or placements the buyer did not agree to.
Record any package that is underdelivering against its intended supply, which can point to targeting that no longer reaches the inventory it names.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ClearLine deal and curated package, the inventory it targets, the inventory the buyer agreed to, and what delivery reporting shows it serving against. A package whose targeting and delivery both match the inventory it was built for passes. A package targeting a renamed or restructured grouping, carrying an unrelated targeting layer, or delivering against inventory the buyer did not agree to is a fail recorded with the package and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each ClearLine package's targeting, the inventory the buyer agreed to, and what it serves against above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more ClearLine deals or curated packages on {{site_name}} target inventory that does not match what the buyer agreed to, so the package delivers against the wrong supply or underdelivers against its intended supply. A curated package is only worth what its inventory mapping promises, and a deal pointing at a renamed grouping or carrying inventory an inventory change swept in spends down the buyer relationship behind it. {{voice_recommend}} opening each active package, comparing its targeting against the inventory it was built for, and reading delivery reporting to confirm what it serves against. After correcting the mappings, recheck delivery to confirm each package reaches the inventory the buyer agreed to.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are left active without being maintained
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package that is never reviewed keeps running against whatever its original targeting still reaches, so as inventory, audience signals, and pricing move, the package drifts from the offer it was built to represent. A stale package underdelivers or delivers diluted supply while still appearing active, which leaves both the buyer relationship and the revenue it carries weaker than the package's status suggests.

educate.base:
A curated package in ClearLine is not a thing a publisher sets once and forgets. The package bundles inventory, an audience or content signal, and a price, and every one of those moves over time. Inventory gets added, renamed, and retired. Audience segments age, and the data behind them loses recency, which is exactly the quality a buyer weighs when deciding whether a segment is still worth activating. Pricing that fit the market when the package launched can sit above or below where comparable inventory clears months later. A package left active without review keeps representing the offer it had on the day it was built, not the offer it actually delivers now.

The drift is quiet because nothing breaks. The package still shows as active, still accepts bids, and still serves. What changes is the gap between what the package promises and what it delivers. A segment that has lost recency reaches fewer of the users it claims. Inventory that was retired leaves the package smaller than its description. A price that no longer fits the market either prices the package out of the buyer's plan or clears it below what the supply is worth. The buyer experiences this as a package that underdelivers or delivers diluted supply, and that experience shapes whether they renew.

Industry data transparency standards exist so buyers can read the recency and provenance of the audience data behind a package, which is the same information a publisher should be checking on the supply side before leaving a package in market. A package whose underlying segments have aged past the freshness a buyer expects is a package that needs refreshing or retiring, not one that should keep running on its original setup.

A few signs point to packages that are not being maintained:
  • Curated packages have been active for many months with no recorded review of their inventory, audience signals, or pricing
  • A package's audience segments rely on data whose recency has aged past what the package description implies
  • Reporting shows a package's reach or delivery shrinking over time while its setup stays unchanged
  • Retired or restructured inventory still appears in a package's scope, so the package describes supply it no longer fully reaches
You may want to set a regular review of each active curated package so its inventory, audience signals, and pricing are checked against current conditions. One approach is to schedule a periodic pass that refreshes the packages worth keeping and retires the ones that no longer represent a current offer.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages often lean on editorial context or seasonal audience signals that age quickly, so a package built around a past campaign or season can keep running long after its context has passed. You may want to review context-based and seasonal packages on a regular schedule, refreshing the ones with a current audience and retiring the ones tied to a moment that has passed.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, curated packages frequently target shopping-intent audiences or product categories whose relevance shifts with the catalog and the buying season. You may want to recheck product-category and intent-based packages periodically so a package built for a category that has shrunk, or a season that has ended, is refreshed or retired rather than left to deliver against thinner supply than it claims.

learn_more.source_title: Bid transparency with the SupplyChain object

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's documentation for reviewing and refreshing ClearLine curated packages is in the partner portal (login required), so the public links above cover the deal and supply-chain transparency standards a publisher checks against rather than the Magnite console steps.

investigate.intro: Review when each active curated package was last checked, and compare its current inventory, audience signals, and pricing against the offer the package was built to represent.

magnite_console.label: Magnite ClearLine console
steps: Open the ClearLine section of the Magnite console and list the active curated packages with their creation or last-edited dates.
For each package, record the inventory, audience signals, and pricing it currently carries, and note any package that has run for many months with no edit.
Check whether the audience segments behind each package still reflect current data and whether the inventory in scope still exists and is reachable.
Flag any package whose inventory has been retired or restructured, or whose audience signal has aged past what the package describes.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by package showing reach or eligible supply and delivery over time.
Compare each package's current reach against its earlier reach to see whether the package is shrinking while its setup stays unchanged.
Note any package whose delivery has fallen off, which can point to inventory or audience signals that have aged out of the package.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per curated package, when it was last reviewed and whether its current inventory, audience signals, and pricing still match the offer it was built to represent. A package reviewed on a regular schedule whose inventory, signals, and pricing remain current passes. A package active for many months with no review, or one whose inventory has been retired, whose audience data has aged, or whose pricing no longer fits the market, is a fail recorded with the package and the dimension that has drifted.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each curated package's last-review date and whether its inventory, audience signals, and pricing are current above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more curated packages on {{site_name}} have been left active without a recorded review, so their inventory, audience signals, and pricing have drifted from the offer the package was built to represent. A package keeps showing as active while its segments age, its inventory is retired, and its pricing falls out of step with the market, which leaves it underdelivering or delivering diluted supply. {{voice_recommend}} setting a regular review of each active package, checking its inventory, audience signals, and pricing against current conditions, and refreshing the packages worth keeping while retiring the ones that no longer represent a current offer. After the review, recheck reach and delivery to confirm each remaining package matches what it claims.
